m_Item1.QuestionText:
struct list
{
int x;
struct list *next;
}  lst,*head=&lst;

head.x = 100;
Whether the above code is correct or wrong?
m_Item1.Explanation: x can be accessed using head->x  or (*head).x  as it is a pointer

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the program ?

#include<stdio.h>
int main ()
{
  enum days   { MON = -1, TUE, WED = 6, THU, FRI, SAT };

  printf ("%d, %d, %d, %d, %d, %d ", MON, TUE, WED, THU, FRI, SAT);
  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
The values inside the enum days are considerd as integers by the compiler. Hence, by default the 1st value is interpreted as 0, 2nd as 1 and so on. But we can assign integer values explicitly to the these enum constants.

In the above program, the value -1 is assigned to MON. Thus, in the next constant TUE, the value 0 is assigned automatically.

Further, the value 6 is assigned to WED. So, automatically,the values assigned in THU, FRI and SAT will be 7, 8 and 9.

Hence, printf() prints these values i.e -1, 0, 6, 7, 8, 9.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A -> B is syntactically correct if?

Options.OptionText: a is a structure and b is a pointer to structure
a is a pointer to structure and b is a structure
a is a pointer to structure in which b is a field

m_Item1.Explanation:
Structure pointer member can also be accessed using -> operator.

(*ptr).a is same as ptr->a
(*ptr).b is same as ptr->b

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main()
{
    enum { WHITE = 0, PURPLE = 4, BLACK, PINK};
    printf("PINK = %d ", PINK);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: PURPLE=4, BLACK =5 so PINK =6. If we do not explicitly assign values to enum names, the compiler by default assigns values starting from 0 and then increases by 1 to next element.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When struct is used instead of the keyword class means, what will happen in the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: The data members in structure are by default public and data members in class are by default private. hence correct answer is A.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following program?
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
typedef struct NType
{
  int I;
  char c;
  long x;
} NewType;

void main ()
{
  NewType *c;
  c = (NewType *) malloc (sizeof (NewType));
  c->I = 100;
  c->c = 'C';
  (*c).x = 100L;
  printf ("%d %c %4Ld", c->I, c->c, c->x);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Structure pointer member can also be accessed using -> operator. (*ptr).a is same as ptr->a (*ptr).b is same as ptr->b

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Code:-
union rainbow
{
int a [5];
float x [5];
};
union rainbow color [20];
void *ptr = color;

Which of the following is the correct way to increment the variable ”ptr” to point to the next member of the array from the sample above?
m_Item1.Explanation: As ptr is void pointer first we need to cast ptr to union rainbow then increment the value by 1 and again convert it to void pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
For the following declaration
union x {
char ch;
int I;
double j;
} u_var;

What is the value of sizeof( u_var)?
m_Item1.Explanation:
An instance of the above union will always take at least a float for storage
It also depends on the alignment that the compiler puts into the allocated space. So, unless you use some special option, the compiler will put padding into your union space.
So to avoid padding into your union use special option and after that the size of the union will always take as much space as the largest men.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <stdio.h>
void main ()
{
  struct s1
  {
    char *z;
    int I;
    struct s1 *p;
  };
 
static struct s1 a[] = { {"Kerala", 6, a + 2}, {"Punjab", 5, a + 1},  {"Delhi", 4, a}  };
 
struct s1 *ptr = a + 1;
 
printf (" %s %s %s", a[0].z, ptr->z, a[2].p->z);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
a[0].z = value of z on 0 index = Kerala
ptr is pointing to a[1] & z of a[1] = Punjab
p of a[2] is pointing to a[0], and  z of a[0] = Kerala.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Union differs from structure in the following way

Options.OptionText: All members are used at a time
Only one member can be used at a time
Union initialized all members as structure

m_Item1.Explanation: With a union, you're only supposed to use one of the elements, because they're all stored at the same spot. This makes it useful when you want to store something that could be one of several types. A struct, on the other hand, has a separate memory location for each of its elements and they all can be used at once.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <stdio.h>
void main ()
{
  struct num1
  {
    char ch[7];
    char *s;
  };
  struct num2
  {
    char *c;
    struct num1 s1;
  };
  static struct num2 s2 = { "apple", "orange", "lemon" };
  printf ("%s %s", s2.c, s2.s1.s);
  printf ("%s %s", s2.c++, ++s2.s1.s);
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
s2.c = "apple".
s2.s1 = {"orange", "lemon"} so s2.s1.s  is lemon .
s2.c++ it will print as it is as post incrementation is used hence it will print apple.
++s2.s1.s  = first increase to next location & then print hence it will print emon .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Point out the correct statement which correctly free the memory pointed to by 's' and 'p' in the following program?
int main()
{
 struct ex
     int i;
     float j;
     char *s
   };
   struct ex *p;
   p = (struct ex *)malloc(sizeof(struct ex));
   p->s = (char*)malloc(20);
   return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: First memory should be deallocated for structure member and then for structure pointer using free() function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following comment about Union is false?

Options.OptionText: Union is a structure whose members share same memory area.
The compiler will keep track of what type of information is currently stored.
Only one of the members of union can be assigned a value at particular time.
Size allocated for Union is the size of its member needing the maximum storage.

m_Item1.Explanation: Compiler does not keep track of what type of information is loaded.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What type of structure is created by the following definition?
struct first { . . . ; struct first *s};
struct second { . . . ; struct second *f};
m_Item1.Explanation: Structure is declaring one of its members as pointer to itself .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
struct customer *ptr = malloc( sizeof( struct customer));

Given the sample allocation for the pointer ”ptr” found, which statement would be used to reallocate ptr to be an array of 10 element?

Options.OptionText: ptr = realloc( ptr, 9 * sizeof( struct customer) );

m_Item1.Explanation: realloc() function needs 2 parameters - original pointer and new size in bytes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of these are valid declaration?
i)   union { int I; int j;};
ii)   union u_tag { int I; int j;} u;
iii)   union { int I; int j; FILE *K};
iv)   union { int I; int j;} u;
m_Item1.Explanation: Union declaration should have atleast name or variable name or both.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the size of ptr1 and ptr2? (16 bit machine)
struct x
{
int j;
char k[ 100];
unsigned I;
};
int *ptr1;
struct x *ptr2;

Options.OptionText: pter1 and ptr2 will have same size.
2, undefined for memory is not allocated.

m_Item1.Explanation: Size of pointer is always equal irrespective of pointer type.

m_Item1.QuestionText: the changes made in the members of a structure are available in the calling function if

Options.OptionText: pointer to structure is passed as argument
the member other then pointer type are passed as argument

m_Item1.Explanation: Changes in the structure will be reflected only when structure pointer is passed because modified value is accessible.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
struct car
  {
    int speed;
    struct car *type;
  } vehicle;
 
  struct car *ptr;
  ptr = &vehicle;

Referring to the code above, which of the following will make the speed equal to 200?
m_Item1.Explanation: When you are using pointer to a structure, speed member will be accessible as ptr->speed or (*ptr).speed.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
struct date
{
int day;
int month;
int year;
};

main()
{
struct date *d;
. . . .
++d -> day; /*statmentN */
. . . .  
}

Then the statement statmentN

Options.OptionText: Increments the pointer to point month.
Increment d by sizeof( struct date).

m_Item1.Explanation: d->day is a int member of structure. So ++ operator increments value present in day .

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following program?
struct xx
{
int a;
long b;
} s;
union yy
{
int a;
long b;
} u;
print sizeof( s ) and sizeof( u ) if sizeof( int ) = 4 and sizeof( long ) = 8.
m_Item1.Explanation: For structure variable size is addtion of all the members and for union variable size is size of largest data member.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
struct
{
int x;
int y;
}abc;
You cannot access x by the following.
1.   abc -> x
2.   abc[0] ->x
3.   abc.x
4.   (abc) ->x
m_Item1.Explanation: x can be accessed by structure variable using . operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
The size of the following union, where an int occupies 4 bytes of memory is
union arc
{
char x;
int y;
char ax[8];
}aha;
m_Item1.Explanation: For union variable memory will be allocated for largest member. x=1 byte, y=4 bytes, ax= 8 bytes. so memory will be allocated to ax i.e 8 bytes which is largest.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the program?
#include <stdio.h>
void main()
{
struct s1 { int i ;};
struct s2 { int i ;};
struct s1 st1;
struct s2 st2;
st1.i = 5;
st2 = st1;
printf("%d", st2.i);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Compilation error. Variable of struct s1 can not be assigned to variable of struct s2. variables of same structure type can be assigned.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider following declaration
      struct DAC
      {
         int  j;
        union
        {
          double ACTS;
           int p;
       } pune;
     }d,*SUN=&d;

   How to access ACTS through SUN?
m_Item1.Explanation: as sun is pointer you can access the pune using -> operator and Acts is part of pune hence you can access that using . operator.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
enum records  {one, two=4, three, four=4};
     
   void main( )
      {
           printf("%d,%d,%d,%d", one, two, three, four);
       }
m_Item1.Explanation: As enum is integral constant the by default value of 1st element of enum is 0 and then it will increase by 1 if not mentioned hence here one is 0 the two is 4 and three is 4+1 i.e 5 and again four is 4. hence output is 0,4,5,4.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not user defined data type?
m_Item1.Explanation: int is standard , predefined, basic datatype.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following correct statement ?

Options.OptionText: Union is a structure whose members share the same storage area.
Size allocated for union is the size of its member needing the maximum storage.
Only one of the members of union can be assigned a value at a particular time.

m_Item1.Explanation: A union is a special data type available in C that allows to store different data types in the same memory location. You can define a union with many members, but only one member can contain a value at any given time. Unions provide an efficient way of using the same memory location for multiple-purpose.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operator is used to access structure member through pointer ?
m_Item1.Explanation: to access a structure member through pointer we have to use '->'.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main( )
      {
        enum days {mon,tue,wed,sat,sun};
         enum days d1,d2,d3;
         d1=mon+sun;
         d2=wed-sat;
         d3=sun*tue;
          printf("%d %d %d",d1,d2,d3);
        }
m_Item1.Explanation:
The values inside the enum days are considerd as integers by the compiler. Hence, by default the 1st value is interpreted as 0, 2nd as 1 and so on.
Hence value of mon will be 0 ,tue =1 , wed =2 , sat = 3, sun =4.
d1= 0+4 i.e 4
d2=2-3 i.e. -1
d3 = 4*1 i.e. 4.
